fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need a small parsing/routing library for the image-copy service — basically a collection of pure helper functions that run before we touch any network or cloud API. The idea is to stop having all that fragile string munging scattered everywhere and make the core logic actually testable in isolation.

We need a few things: something to pull the cloud region out of a registry hostname (you know, the managed container registry URLs we use), a credential classifier so we know whether to go fetch a secret or just use the value directly, and a parser for those object-storage archive location strings we use for image archives in the bucket system. The archive transport thing also needs to expose its scheme identifier and should flat-out reject any policy scope you throw at it — that transport just doesn't support scopes, period.

Also need a utility that reads a stream but hard-caps how much it buffers — we had that incident last quarter where an oversized payload caused memory issues, so this is important.

For the docker reference normalization, just follow the same convention we use in the login module — you'll know it when you see it in the codebase. The output format should be the same key=value style we already use in the other adapters. Make sure the test harness can be pointed at different case directories without overwriting previous runs.

trigger_keywords: default region when no ECR hostname
what region is returned if URI doesn't match
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the URI does not contain a recognizable ECR hostname pattern (<account>.dkr.ecr.<region>.amazonaws.com), the function must report a fixed default region. The spec does not pin the exact string — it just must be a consistent, hardcoded fallback — but the output line format is always region=<value>.

trigger_keywords: why does ARN win over colon check
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The three credential categories are checked in strict priority order: (1) if the string starts with 'arn:aws', classify as SECRET_ARN; (2) else if the string contains a colon, classify as SECRET_TEXT; (3) otherwise (including empty string), classify as SECRET_NAME. An ARN always wins even though it also contains colons — the prefix check must come first.

trigger_keywords: how to parse the bucket and key
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The archive reference format is //<bucket>[/<key>][:<suffix>]. The text immediately after '//' up to the first '/' is the bucket. Everything after that first '/' and before the first ':' is the key. A trailing slash with no key yields an empty key. The suffix after the first ':' is either a docker image reference or a source index prefixed with '@'.

trigger_keywords: how bare image names get expanded
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A docker reference embedded in the archive reference must be normalized to its canonical fully-qualified repository form: short names like 'busybox' expand to 'docker.io/library/busybox:latest'. A missing tag always defaults to 'latest'. References carrying a content digest (e.g., @sha256:...) are rejected and cause the entire parse to return valid=false.

trigger_keywords: mutual exclusion of docker ref and index
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A source index is introduced by '@' in the suffix (e.g., :@0). It must be zero or positive — a negative value (e.g., :@-1) causes valid=false. A docker reference and a source index are mutually exclusive: supplying both in either order must also yield valid=false. When neither is supplied, source_index=-1 is reported as a sentinel and ref=(none).

trigger_keywords: what identifier does the S3 transport use
scheme string for object storage transport
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The object-storage archive transport registers under the fixed scheme identifier 's3'. The transport_name operation always outputs exactly: name=s3

trigger_keywords: what happens when scope is passed
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The object-storage transport supports no named policy-configuration scopes whatsoever — not even the empty default scope. Every call to policy_scope must output accepted=false followed by error=scope_not_supported, regardless of what scope string is supplied.

trigger_keywords: is the size limit inclusive or exclusive
what happens when size exactly equals limit
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The size limit is inclusive: a stream whose size is exactly equal to the limit succeeds (ok=true, bytes_read=<size>). Only a stream whose size strictly exceeds the limit fails (ok=false, error=size_exceeded, limit=<limit>). One byte over the limit is the failure threshold.

The test harness writes one file per case to rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename.stem}@{case_index.zfill(3)}.txt. Output is namespaced by the cases-dir value so different runs never overwrite each other. Each .txt file contains only the raw stdout from the program under test — no PASS/FAIL summaries or metadata.

trigger_keywords: what directory does test.sh use by default
how to point test harness at different cases
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The test script rcb_tests/test.sh defaults to reading cases from rcb_tests/public_test_cases/. It accepts a --cases-dir <subdir> argument to point at a different directory of *.json case files. The stdout output directory mirrors whatever cases-dir value is active.

trigger_keywords: what if ref string is empty
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: An empty ref string is explicitly listed as a rejection condition for parse_reference. It must output valid=false with no additional fields.

trigger_keywords: how is the region extracted from the hostname
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The region is extracted by matching the pattern .dkr.ecr.<region>.amazonaws.com anywhere within the URI string — scheme, path, and tag are all ignored. The region segment is the single dot-separated token sitting between the 'dkr.ecr.' label and the '.amazonaws.com' suffix.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid op field values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which op strings does the adapter support
what are the possible op inputs
tell me all the op names
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'op' field. Valid values are: 'ecr_region', 'creds_type', 'parse_reference', 'transport_name', 'policy_scope', 'read_limit'. Each value maps to one feature.

trigger_keywords: test cases for feature1 ecr region
show me feature1 ecr region test cases
what are the feature1 ecr region test inputs
give me the feature1_ecr_region test file
what does feature1 ecr region expect
test cases for feature2 creds type
show me feature2 creds type test cases
what are the feature2 creds type test inputs
give me the feature2_creds_type test file
what does feature2 creds type expect
test cases for feature3 1 reference parse
show me feature3 1 reference parse test cases
what are the feature3 1 reference parse test inputs
give me the feature3_1_reference_parse test file
what does feature3 1 reference parse expect
test cases for feature3 2 transport name
show me feature3 2 transport name test cases
what are the feature3 2 transport name test inputs
give me the feature3_2_transport_name test file
what does feature3 2 transport name expect
test cases for feature3 3 policy scope
show me feature3 3 policy scope test cases
what are the feature3 3 policy scope test inputs
give me the feature3_3_policy_scope test file
what does feature3 3 policy scope expect
test cases for feature4 read limit
show me feature4 read limit test cases
what are the feature4 read limit test inputs
give me the feature4_read_limit test file
what does feature4 read limit expect

context_pointers.vague_reference: just follow the same convention we use in the login module
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Docker reference normalization logic: expand bare short names to docker.io/library/<name>:latest, always default missing tags to 'latest', and reject digest-bearing references — as specified in the parse_reference contract in feature 3.1

context_pointers.vague_reference: the same key=value style we already use in the other adapters
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The stdout output format: one key=value pair per line, each line newline-terminated, exactly matching the expected_output strings in the JSON test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/

context_pointers.vague_reference: that incident last quarter where an oversized payload caused memory issues
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The read_limit feature (Feature 4): a size-bounded stream read that reports ok=false with error=size_exceeded and limit=<limit> when the stream size exceeds the configured limit

The object-storage archive transport (Feature 3): references of the form //<bucket>[/<key>][:<suffix>] used to locate image archives in S3-compatible object storage, parsed by the parse_reference operation

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have more specific details on that aspect — please check the existing test cases under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ and the inline comments in the codebase for authoritative behavior. If it's about output formatting, match exactly what the expected_output fields show in the JSON test files.

feature1_ecr_region.json.description: Given a container-registry URI string, extract the cloud region embedded in an Elastic Container Registry hostname. The hostname has the shape <account>.dkr.ecr.<region>.amazonaws.com, and the region is the segment located between the registry-service label and the cloud domain suffix. The output reports the extracted region name. When the URI does not contain such a hostname, a fixed default region is reported instead.

feature2_creds_type.json.description: Classify a credential string into one of three domain categories used to decide how the credential should later be resolved. A value that begins with the well-known cloud resource-name prefix is classified as a stored-secret ARN reference. Otherwise, a value that contains a colon is classified as inline secret text (a literal username/password style value). Any remaining value, including the empty string, is classified as a stored-secret name. The output reports the category label.

feature3_1_reference_parse.json.description: Parse a location reference for an image archive stored in object storage. The reference has the form //<bucket>[/<key>][:<suffix>]: everything up to the first colon names a storage bucket and an optional object key (the key is empty when omitted, and a trailing slash with no key also yields an empty key). The optional suffix after the first colon is either a docker image reference (a repository with an optional tag) or a zero-based source index introduced by an '@'. A successful parse reports the bucket, the object key, the normalized docker reference, and the source index. A docker reference is normalized to its canonical fully-qualified repository form, and a missing tag defaults to the conventional 'latest' tag; when no docker reference is supplied a none-marker is reported. When no source index is supplied a sentinel negative value is reported. The reference is rejected as invalid when it is empty, when the embedded docker reference is malformed, when it carries a content digest, when a docker reference and a source index are supplied together, or when the source index is negative.

feature3_2_transport_name.json.description: Report the scheme name advertised by the object-storage image transport. The transport registers itself under a short, fixed scheme identifier that callers use to select it when routing a reference. The output reports that identifier.

feature3_3_policy_scope.json.description: Validate a policy-configuration scope string for the object-storage image transport. This transport does not support any named policy scopes: every scope value, including the empty default scope, is rejected. The output reports that the scope was not accepted together with a neutral not-supported error category, independent of the specific scope string supplied.

feature4_read_limit.json.description: Read an entire input stream into memory while enforcing an upper bound on how many bytes may be copied, guarding against unbounded memory use. The request supplies the number of bytes available in the stream and the maximum number of bytes permitted. When the available size is less than or equal to the limit the read succeeds and reports the number of bytes read. When the available size exceeds the limit the read fails and reports a neutral size-exceeded error together with the limit that was applied.
